annotations.text: Sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia can be seen in patients with virtually any disease affecting the myocardium and can even be seen infrequently in the absence of overt structural heart disease.
The presence of sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is often associated with a poor outcome in patients with heart disease.

One syndrome that deserves mention in this case is arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia, which is primarily a right ventricular cardiomyopathy, frequently with little or no left ventricular involvement.
Although this patient seems to have had disproportionate right ventricular involvement, other clinical findings would not be explained by this diagnosis.
In this case ventricular tachycardia was occurring in the setting of a global cardiomyopathy or myocarditis.
Cardiomyopathies comprise dilated, hypertrophic, and restrictive types.
The characteristic echocardiographic findings include increased wall thickness without chamber enlargement and evidence of abnormal diastolic function with ultimate systolic dysfunction.
In view of the presence of thoracic lymphadenopathy in this young nonsmoker, the possibility of lymphoma should be considered.
Cardiac involvement, which is found at autopsy in up to 24 percent of patients with lymphoma, is typically seen in the form of extension to the parietal pericardium, with or without effusions.
The diagnosis of sarcoidosis should be strongly considered in any young patient with conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia.
Sarcoidosis is a systemic granulomatous disorder of unknown cause characterized by noncaseating epithelioid-cell granulomas involving multiple organ systems.
Although tuberculous myocarditis is a possibility in this case, several findings provide evidence against this diagnosis.
Generalized anergy is more consistent with lymphoma or sarcoidosis.
The pattern of lung involvement, although atypical, is consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
Finally, the cardiomyopathy, conduction-system dysfunction, and sustained ventricular tachycardia are common manifestations of cardiac sarcoidosis.

The presentation may also include myocarditis with congestive heart failure, sudden cardiac death, supraventricular tachyarrhythmia, mitral-valve insufficiency due to papillary-muscle dysfunction, formation of a left ventricular aneurysm, acute myocardial infarction, and pericarditis.
The relative frequency of cardiac involvement in sarcoidosis varies according to race.
Death from pulmonary sarcoidosis is more common than death from cardiac sarcoidosis in blacks, whereas the converse is true in Asians.
Patients with pulmonary sarcoidosis and cardiac symptoms are generally assumed to have sarcoidosis of the heart.

Cor pulmonale may develop because of pulmonary fibrosis or selective vascular obliteration by granulomas, but it is relatively uncommon and has not developed in this patient despite radiologic evidence of extensive disease.
After a course of immunosuppressant therapy, his left ventricular function returned to normal, and the pulmonary sarcoidosis is less active.
